hypothesis: The computational cost of HMC denoising in AMDEN scales prohibitively with system size because gradient evaluations through the energy-based score function must be performed at every Verlet integration step; therefore, training a lightweight surrogate interatomic potential (e.g., a machine-learning force field) on intermediate denoising trajectories generated by the full AMDEN model and using this surrogate exclusively to drive HMC refinement steps—while reserving the full EGNN only for score updates at coarser diffusion intervals—will reduce inference wall-clock time by at least an order of magnitude without statistically significant degradation in radial distribution function accuracy or Young's modulus prediction error compared to the unmodified HMC denoising procedure.
